advantages: Lets you tune the same algorithm for very different deployments (mobile vs server, prototype vs prod).
Many huge wins are a one-line change (`@lru_cache`, `set(...)`, prefix sums).
Provides a clean vocabulary for design discussions ('we'll pay 200 MB to halve p99').
Often the difference between 'doesn't fit' and 'fits comfortably'.
disadvantages: Caches and memoization add complexity — invalidation, eviction, staleness.
Memory you 'save' may push GC, swap, or eviction problems elsewhere.
Hidden costs: an O(n) hash table has constants that may lose at small n.
Hard to revert once a system is built around a particular tradeoff (e.g. quantized weights).
